summary: Lithuania's posture is led by the National Cyber Security Centre (NKSC), which presents the transition to post-quantum cryptography as one of the most important near-term decisions for the country's digital resilience. Its guidance directs organisations to begin with a cryptographic inventory and to prepare for the move to quantum-resistant algorithms, while its annual reporting follows the standardisation of these algorithms and the NIST post-quantum project. No national migration deadline has been published.
govActors: NKSC | National Cyber Security Centre of Lithuania, sets national guidance on post-quantum cryptography and cryptographic inventory
provenance: NKSC lead and PQC as a key near-term decision: NKSC overview of cybersecurity status and NKSC news item on post-quantum cryptography (ingested)
Cryptographic inventory as the starting step: NKSC guidelines for cryptography use inventory and NKSC Cyberpusryciai PQC presentation (ingested)
Tracking standardisation of quantum-resistant algorithms and the NIST PQC project: NKSC CTAC 2024 Yearly Report (ingested)
PQC deployment funding via Digital Europe Programme: NKSC info-day presentation 2024 (ingested)

dominantProcess omitted: the NIST PQC project is discussed in the yearly report as the standardisation reference, but no document adopts the NIST FIPS suite as Lithuanian national guidance, so the camp is not clearly attested - REVIEW
algorithms/standardFamilies/hybridDeployment/migrationTimeline/targetCompletion omitted: not stated in captured summaries
EU-aligned by membership: participates in the NIS Cooperation Group coordinated PQC roadmap (high-risk by 2030, full by 2035); the timeline shown is the EU coordinated roadmap, not a national one.
Model 2026-06-14 (EU / taker, High): EU coordinated roadmap (NIS Cooperation Group); adopts the NIST baseline as a taker. No national algorithm.
migrationTimeline:
2030 | High-risk use cases migrated
2035 | Full migration of all systems complete

mainRegulation:
NIS2 Directive (EU) 2022/2555, Art. 21(2)(h) | EU | binding-law
DORA, Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 (financial sector) | EU | binding-law
Commission Recommendation (EU) 2024/1101 on PQC | EU | soft-law

obligation: Essential and important entities must use state-of-the-art cryptography under NIS2; migration follows the EU coordinated 2026/2030/2035 timeline. No distinct national post-quantum instrument is recorded in the Atlas sources.
analyticalNote: Lithuania is bound by the EU regulatory spine (NIS2, DORA) and follows the coordinated roadmap; no national algorithm or dedicated post-quantum statute is recorded, so it adopts the NIST baseline under EU coordination. Watch point: national transposition and any country-specific guidance would refine this EU-floor entry.
